text:
The growth of lithium dendrites in rechargeable lithium-ion and lithium-metal batteries presents a major obstacle to their safety and longevity. Developing effective mitigation strategies requires a deep, fundamental understanding of how these structures evolve under real-world operating conditions. In this study, we utilize in situ $^7$Li magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) to simultaneously monitor the electrolyte concentration gradients and the morphological changes of lithium metal electrodes within symmetric cells. 

Our approach relies on chemical shift (CS) imaging, a highly sensitive diagnostic tool that differentiates between various lithium microstructures based on their distinct signatures. We observed that mossy lithium deposits begin to form at the electrode surface almost immediately upon charging in every cell studied. In contrast, true dendritic growth—which spans the gap between electrodes—is triggered only after a specific delay. To quantify these processes, we developed simple metrics to track dendrite initiation and electrolyte depletion across a range of current densities.

The results reveal two distinct growth regimes. At high charge rates, we found a strong correlation between the onset of dendrite growth and the local depletion of lithium salts at the electrode surface, a phenomenon both captured experimentally and predicted by the theoretical Sand’s time model. However, at low current densities, dendrites emerge much earlier than theory suggests, indicating a separate growth mechanism that is not governed by bulk salt depletion. By providing a direct link between solid-state electrode morphology and liquid-phase concentration changes, this MRI-based methodology offers a robust framework for investigating and controlling dendrite formation in next-generation batteries.
